Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only executes global variable definitions and declares the `source` array when sourced. There is no `pkgver()` function or any command substitution in global scope that would run code. The `source` array fetches a pinned release from the official upstream GitHub repository (`github.com/pingdotgg/t3code/releases/download/...`), plus two local files (`t3code-icon.png`, `LICENSE`). All sources have pinned SHA-256 checksums. There are no suspicious network requests, obfuscated commands, or data exfiltration attempts. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe because no potentially dangerous code (in `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()`) is executed at this stage.
</details>
<summary>Safe: only variable definitions and standard source downloads.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The wrapper script sets necessary environment variables and passes `--no-sandbox` flags (common for Electron apps in non-sandboxed environments). No evidence of malicious behavior such as data exfiltration, hidden network requests, or obfuscated code is present.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR nightly bin packaging; no security concerns.</summary>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/t3code-nightly-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-l0cn_d9e...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-l0cn_d9e
Analyzing t3code-nightly-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-l0cn_d9e) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources: a pinned release asset from GitHub (using a verifiable tag and checksum), a.png icon, and a LICENSE file. The `sha256sums` array includes valid checksums for all sources, and the AppImage URL uses string interpolation with variables defined in the global scope, which is safe and common practice. During `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`, only the global scope and `pkgver()` (not present) are evaluated — no function bodies are executed. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions, which contain `chmod`, `--appimage-extract`, `install`, `cp`, and here-doc scripts, are not run due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`, so their contents are out of scope for this narrow gate. Merely sourcing the PKGBUILD poses no risk of code execution or data exfiltration, as there is no command substitution, network access, or eval code in the top-level scope. The behavior is consistent with standard, safe AUR packaging.
</details>
<summary>
Sources safe; no execution during fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Sources safe; no execution during fetch[/]

makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, LICENSE...
LLM auditresponse for LICENSE:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file provided is a LICENSE file containing the standard text of the MIT License. It includes a copyright notice for T3 Tools Inc. and the year 2026, which may be a forward-dated typo or placeholder but is not inherently suspicious in the context of licensing. The content is plain legal text with no executable code, scripts, encoded payloads, or network-related instructions. It does not perform any file operations, system modifications, or data exfiltration. The presence of properly escaped HTML entities like " is consistent with text formatting and poses no risk. This file serves only to declare licensing terms and contains no security threats.
</details>
<summary>
Standard MIT license with no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed LICENSE. Status: SAFE -- Standard MIT license with no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves an AppImage from the project's official GitHub repository under the <https://github.com/pingdotgg/t3code> organization, which is consistent with the package's stated purpose. The checksums are provided and match the expected hashes for the source files, including the AppImage and auxiliary files like the icon and license. The `prepare()` function extracts the AppImage using standard `--appimage-extract`, which is a known and safe method when applied to a verified binary.

The `package()` function installs the extracted payload into `/opt`, creates a wrapper script to set appropriate environment variables (such as `PATH`, `XDG_DATA_DIRS`, and Wayland support flags), and sets up desktop integration. The wrapper script does not exhibit obfuscation or unauthorized network activity. It conditionally sets `CODEX_CLI_PATH` if the `codex` command is available, which relates to optional dependency functionality and is within expected application behavior. All operations are confined to the package directory, and standard desktop integration steps (icons, MIME, desktop file) are performed.

There are no signs of code injection, unexpected downloads, or data exfiltration. The use of `chmod`, `cp`, `install`, and `ln` is standard for AUR packaging. While the AppImage is not rebuilt from source, this is expected for a `-bin` package distributing a precompiled upstream artifact. The overall build process follows safe and conventional practices for packaging a third-party binary.
</details>
<summary>
Standard packaging of verified upstream AppImage; no malicious code found.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard packaging of verified upstream AppImage; no malicious code found.
Reviewed all required files.

Reviewing additional files...
Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing .upstream-commit...
LLM auditresponse for .upstream-commit:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `.upstream-commit` contains a single line with a Git commit hash: `43db5b0f3fea2c4ac0a075c988985f6c453c4396`. This type of file is commonly used in AUR packages, especially `-git` or development versions, to track the specific upstream commit the package is based on. It poses no security risk as it is purely informational and contains no executable code, network operations, or file manipulations.

There are no suspicious commands, obfuscated content, or malicious behaviors present. The file does not perform any actions by itself and is typically read by helper scripts to verify or update the source checkout. Such practices are standard in AUR packaging workflows and consistent with transparent, reproducible builds.

No evidence of supply-chain tampering or injected malicious logic was found. The content is static, readable, and within expected norms for version tracking in AUR packages.
</details>
<summary>
Tracking file with Git commit hash, no executable code</summary>
</security_assessment>
